did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a skin disease detection tool using machine learning and deep learning techniques, specifically comparing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Random Forests (RF). It trained models on a dataset of 280 images (initially 4 classes, then split into 8 classes) to classify skin diseases, achieving an accuracy of 90.02% for the 4-class test and 88.5% for the 8-class CNN model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors developed a skin disease detection tool using Python, machine learning, and deep learning. It details a specific methodology involving image preprocessing, feature extraction, and the implementation and comparison of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Random Forests (RF) models. The study reports specific results, including accuracy metrics (90.02% for 4-class and 88.5% for 8-class tests) on a dataset of 280 images, which constitutes new empirical results rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: SKIN DISEASE DETECTION USING PYTHON AND DEEP LEARNING
journal: International Journal of Engineering Applied Sciences and Technology
brief_summary: This study developed a non-invasive skin disease detection tool using Python, machine learning, and deep learning to address the difficulty of early diagnosis by non-specialists. The authors implemented and compared Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Random Forests (RF) algorithms on a custom dataset of 280 images covering four initial disease classes. The dataset was subsequently split into eight classes to evaluate multiclass classification performance. Evaluation results showed that the CNN model achieved an accuracy of 90.02% in the 4-class test and 88.5% in the 8-class test, outperforming other multiclass algorithms which failed to exceed 50% accuracy. The findings suggest that the proposed deep learning approach can serve as a reliable, low-cost aid for early skin disease detection.

all_task_types.details: The paper implements a skin disease detection tool using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Random Forests (RF) to classify skin diseases into discrete classes. The study reports performance on a 4-class test (accuracy 90.02%) and an 8-class test (accuracy 88.5%) using a custom dataset of 280 images. The task is explicitly defined as categorizing illnesses/diseases.

datasets_used.name: Custom Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of skin disease images collected by the authors for the study. The paper does not specify the source (e.g., hospital, public repository) or the specific diseases included in the 4-class and 8-class splits, only that it consists of images of diseased skin.

use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate model accuracy (90.02% for 4-class test, 88.5% for 8-class test).

Custom Skin Disease Dataset (4-class spl.notes: The dataset consisted of 280 images. The CNN model was tested with a 4-class test configuration.

Custom Skin Disease Dataset (8-class spl.notes: The dataset images were split into 8 classes to evaluate multiclass classification performance. The CNN achieved 88.5% accuracy, while other multiclass classification algorithms failed to exceed 50% accuracy.

conclusions: The proposed non-invasive skin disease detection tool using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Random Forests (RF) is accurate and easy-to-use for identifying appropriate skin conditions.
The CNN model achieved an accuracy of 90.02% in the 4-class test and 88.5% in the 8-class test, outperforming other multiclass classification algorithms which failed to exceed 50% accuracy.
The study demonstrates that skin diseases can be detected with a very low percentage error using high-precision algorithms and image model training.
The proposed tool can serve as a reliable aid for early skin disease detection, potentially saving money and providing a second opinion for patients.
Future improvements include identifying all sorts of skin diseases using a similar paradigm, supporting multilingualism to improve usability, and increasing multi-platform compatibility (e.g., iOS).
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, custom dataset of only 280 images, which significantly limits the generalizability and robustness of the deep learning models.
The specific classes for the 4-class and 8-class splits are not explicitly defined in the text, creating ambiguity regarding the exact diseases evaluated.
The dataset source is not specified (e.g., hospital, public repository), raising concerns about potential selection bias and lack of external validation.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported accuracy differences between models.
The paper does not report the train/validation/test split ratios, making it impossible to assess data leakage risks or overfitting.
The CNN architecture details (specific layer counts, filter sizes, backbone) are not provided, preventing reproducibility.
The Random Forest model used Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features, while the CNN used raw pixels; this methodological inconsistency complicates direct comparison.
The claim that 'other multiclass classification algorithms failed to exceed 50% accuracy' lacks detail on which specific algorithms were tested and their configurations.
The study lacks comparison with state-of-the-art pre-trained models (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet) or large-scale public dermatology datasets (e.g., HAM10000, ISIC).
The paper acknowledges the need for future work on multi-platform compatibility and multilingualism, indicating the current tool is not a deployed clinical product.
